text: We introduce MCTest: a large, open-domain machine comprehension dataset in the form of a set of stories and corresponding question. We describe the challenges and design decisions that have to be considered in order to create a large high quality dataset. Previous work on the machine comprehension (MC) task such as modeling semantic knowledge bases given a few examples has shown promising advancements but is still limited due to one of the following two constraints: either they are limited to small and controlled datasets such as lists of attributes and concrete examples for few specific categories of objects, or they restrict the comprehension goal to relatively small or narrow-scope subsets of human understandable language, such as open-domain relation extraction and otherMC variants that require answers to specific types of single sentences. In contrast, reading comprehension is meant to encompass all of the more complex knowledge that is thought to be required for human comprehension of a story. Even though the task has the advantage of clearly identifying the correct answer, it may also test abilities such as causality, understanding the physical world described by the passage, etc. Finally, since the answers in the fiction stories can only be inferred by being grounded in the text itself (unlike text about real life) we focus on that aspect and build the task of reading comprehension as a MC test on relatively long (a page of text) fictional passages, where the answers can be inferred only by reading and interpreting the passages given as input. Furthermore, in order to ensure that children can understand and therefore base their inference on something that they have actually read, we restrict the comprehension goal to passages of text no more complex than those that a young child could read and understand. The full dataset consists of 500 test stories with four choice questions each about a 6 sentence story, the full set can be easily and affordably (at a cost that can be reduced to less than a dollar per sample) be made scalable to produce thousands of new such examples to ensure that any method overfits to a small portion of the training data.
explanation: The passage reads like a formal research summary with domain-specific details about MCTest, which is more characteristic of human authorship, though advanced AI could imitate this style.
